// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 1 — TECHNOLOGY IN EVERYDAY LIFE AND BUSINESS

Let me show you the cycle with something concrete.

Chapter 1 — Technology in Everyday Life — A payroll clerk at a small company sits down on Friday. She opens a spreadsheet. The spreadsheet contains employee names
NARRATION

Let me show you the cycle with something concrete. A payroll clerk at a small company sits down on Friday. She opens a spreadsheet. The spreadsheet contains employee names in column A and hours worked in column B. She types the number 42 into cell B7 , that is Marcus's hours for the week. She moves to the formula in column C, which multiplies hours by the hourly wage. She hits Enter. The number 1,344.00 appears in C7. She saves the file. She sends it to the HR system. Done.

// SLIDE 02 — THE TWO THINGS INSIDE THE BOX

THE TWO THINGS INSIDE THE BOX.

—
CPU — Hardware
RAM — Hardware
Hard Drive — Hardware
NARRATION

Now open the box. Not literally , though it is educational to open a computer once in your life and look at the parts. What you would find are two categories of thing, and the distinction between them is one of the most useful in all of computing. Hardware is the physical stuff. The circuit board that everything mounts on , the motherboard. The processor chip , the CPU. The memory chips , RAM. The storage drive. The screen. The keyboard. The power supply.

// SLIDE 03 — THE WIRE THAT CHANGED EVERYTHI

THE WIRE THAT CHANGED EVERYTHING.

—
Speed Of Access — Speed Of Access — local storage applied to the chapter example.
Control — Control — local storage applied to the chapter example.
Available Without Internet — Available Without Internet — local storage applied to the chapter example.
NARRATION

Everything so far describes a single computer doing work by itself. But almost no computer works alone anymore. The moment you connect a computer to a network , even just the wifi in a coffee shop , the picture changes enormously. A network is just computers connected together so they can share data. A local network , the wifi in your office or home , is called a LAN, local area network. It might connect twenty or thirty devices: laptops, phones, printers, maybe a shared storage device.

// SLIDE 04 — PUTTING IT TOGETHER

PUTTING IT TOGETHER.

You now have the stack. Four operations at the bottom: input, processing, storage, output. Two categories of stuff insid

Putting It Together — When something breaks, you ask: which layer? When something is slow, you ask: which step? When you are making a decision
NARRATION

You now have the stack. Four operations at the bottom: input, processing, storage, output. Two categories of stuff inside the box: hardware and software, with the operating system managing the boundary. And above that, a network that makes location irrelevant and the cloud that puts professional infrastructure at the end of your wifi connection.

// SLIDE 05 — CHAPTER SUMMARY

CHAPTER SUMMARY

input→processing→storage→output→Hardware
NARRATION

Every computer, in every context, performs four operations: input (data enters), processing (the CPU operates on data following instructions), storage (data is saved temporarily in RAM or permanently on disk), and output (results go back out). These four operations are the skeleton underneath everything computing does. Inside the box, two categories of thing do the work. Hardware is physical , the CPU, RAM, hard drive, motherboard. Software is instructions , programs, operating systems, applications.

// SLIDE 06 — EXERCISES

EXERCISES

1.1→1.2→1.3→1.4→1.5
NARRATION

1.1 (The processing cycle.) A manager asks her assistant to pull up last quarter's sales report, add the new October figures, and email the updated file to the team. List at least five distinct actions in this sequence. For each, identify whether it is input, processing, storage, or output. If one action fits two categories, explain why. 1.2 (Hardware vs.

// SLIDE 07 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

·Ask it to explain what the spanning tree protocol actually does in plain language, using the analogy of a city's road sy
Ask"Perlman is called the 'Mother of the Internet' but says she dislikes the title. What's the work she'd rather be known f
Add the constraint"Answer as if you're a network engineer explaining to a finance team why their VPN went down at 9 a.m."
NARRATION

The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Radia Perlman was working on the spanning tree protocol that makes the modern internet's link layer work , the unseen plumbing underneath every "the network is down" frustration you've ever had decades before most people had heard of computing's network layer. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.

NARRATION

NARRATION

Every computer, in every context, performs four operations: input (data enters), processing (the CPU operates on data following instructions), storage (data is saved temporarily in RAM or permanently on disk), and output (results go back out). These four operations are the skeleton underneath everything computing does. Inside the box, two categories of thing do the work. Hardware is physical , the CPU, RAM, hard drive, motherboard.
